channel.title: idea on Daniels page
channel.description: Recent content in idea on Daniels page

item.title: Using if else with pipes
item.description:
A nice way to use if else within pipes is (see here):

library(magrittr)
runif(1) %>% magrittr::is_greater_than(0.5) %>% {if(.) "foo" else "bar"}
## [1] "bar"

Alternatively, the when function from the purrr package can be used:

library(purrr, quietly = TRUE, warn.conflicts = FALSE)
runif(1) %>% purrr::when(. <= 0.5 ~ "foo", 
                         . >  0.5 ~ "bar") 
## [1] "bar"

A nice way to use a vector of inputs is provided by the case_when function from dplyr together with mutate or transmute:

library(dplyr,  quietly = TRUE, warn.conflicts = FALSE)
library(tibble, quietly = TRUE, warn.conflicts = FALSE)
runif(100) %>% 
  magrittr::is_greater_than(0.5) %>% 
  tibble::tibble(a = .) %>% 
  dplyr::transmute(., foobar = case_when(a <= 0.5 ~ "fu", a >  0.5 ~ "bar"))
## # A tibble: 100 x 1
##    foobar
##    <chr> 
##  1 fu    
##  2 fu    
##  3 bar   
##  4 fu    
##  5 fu    
##  6 fu    
##  7 bar   
##  8 bar   
##  9 fu    
## 10 fu    
## # ... with 90 more rows

item.title: Using if else with pipes
item.description:
A nice way to use if else within pipes is (see here):

library(magrittr)
runif(1) %>% magrittr::is_greater_than(0.5) %>% {if(.) "foo" else "bar"}
## [1] "bar"

Alternatively, the when function from the purrr package can be used:

library(purrr, quietly = TRUE, warn.conflicts = FALSE)
runif(1) %>% purrr::when(. <= 0.5 ~ "foo", 
                         . >  0.5 ~ "bar") 
## [1] "bar"

A nice way to use a vector of inputs is provided by the case_when function from dplyr together with mutate or transmute:

library(dplyr,  quietly = TRUE, warn.conflicts = FALSE)
library(tibble, quietly = TRUE, warn.conflicts = FALSE)
runif(100) %>% 
  magrittr::is_greater_than(0.5) %>% 
  tibble::tibble(a = .) %>% 
  dplyr::transmute(., foobar = case_when(a <= 0.5 ~ "fu", a >  0.5 ~ "bar"))
## # A tibble: 100 x 1
##    foobar
##    <chr> 
##  1 fu    
##  2 fu    
##  3 fu    
##  4 fu    
##  5 fu    
##  6 fu    
##  7 bar   
##  8 bar   
##  9 bar   
## 10 bar   
## # ... with 90 more rows

item.title: Feedback with tribbles
item.description:
The ordering and display of tables in Rmarkdown (aka data.frames or tibbles) has become so good. I actually prefer it over using print right now. Especially, because I can format in in an intuitive way by using the tribble() function. Here is an example. Let us say I want to summarize the basic set up for an optimization. Some dummy variables might look like this:

library(tibble)

number_iterations <- 1000
stoping_criterion <- -1
stepsize <- 0.5
sensitivity <- 0.2

To give a (nicely formatted) summary in the past I would have done something like this:

library(pasta)

print("number of iterations:" %&&% number_iterations)
## [1] "number of iterations: 1000"
print("stoping criterion:" %&&% stoping_criterion)
## [1] "stoping criterion: -1"
print("step size:" %&&% stepsize)
## [1] "step size: 0.5"
print("sensitivity:" %&&% sensitivity)
## [1] "sensitivity: 0.2"

Now, the tribble functions allows me to order the feedback in a way that is nicely formatted in code and in display:

info <- tribble(
  ~criterion,             ~value,
  "number of iterations", number_iterations,
  "stoping criterion",    stoping_criterion,
  "step size",            stepsize,
  "sensitivity",          sensitivity
)

info
## # A tibble: 4 x 2
##              criterion  value
##                  <chr>  <dbl>
## 1 number of iterations 1000.0
## 2    stoping criterion   -1.0
## 3            step size    0.5
## 4          sensitivity    0.2
